core.title: RESILIAGE Atlas
core.tagline: WebGIS-based MultiAtlas for exploring spatial and non-spatial resilience, risk, and cultural-natural heritage data.
core.teamContext: Politecnico di Torino RESILIAGE team member; owned end-to-end implementation, deployment, data ingestion coordination, and database operations from partner-provided initial design direction

story.summary: A full-stack Atlas designed for an EU Horizon Europe consortium to explore community-environment relationships through cultural, environmental, agricultural, demographic, heritage, DRR, crowdsourced, and simulation-based data.
story.problem: Stakeholders needed one geographic workspace for interpreting resilience across many evidence types, from cultural-natural assets and historical routes to risk areas, local living heritage, survey testimonies, crowdsourced media, and 3D simulations.
story.solution: A flexible WebGIS platform around structured Atlas content, synchronized exploration flows, and a dual-format mapping pipeline: PMTiles for lightweight vector overlays and an async FastAPI tile proxy for Cloud-Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs). I coupled this with state-synchronized legends, an event-driven onboarding guide, and secure AWS S3 asset delivery.
story.outcome: A multidimensional resilience material into a navigable WebGIS workspace where users can explore heritage, environment, risk, community narratives, and simulations through a shared geographic lens.
highlights: Architected a dynamic raster tile proxy using FastAPI and TiTiler to stream, style, and project Cloud-Optimized GeoTIFFs (COGs) hosted on S3 on-the-fly.
Engineered client-side vector styling with MapLibre GL by generating dynamic 'match' expressions compiled directly from database-configured symbology configurations.
Developed a state-aware interactive tour using React Joyride that monitors active UI states (sidebar toggles, region selection, layer loads) to auto-advance steps.
Created a unified layer-management system with customized legends supporting continuous color-ramps, discrete category cards, and cyclic variables (e.g. aspect angle).
Secured private project assets (high-res cartography maps and pilot site simulation videos) using AWS S3 short-lived presigned URLs generated dynamically on-demand.
lessons: Building high-performance geospatial web tools requires tile-based delivery with formats like COGs and PMTiles, so the client only requests the data needed for the current viewport.

Highly complex scientific dashboards require active progressive disclosure; connecting tutorial events directly to application state ensures users are onboarded naturally without getting overwhelmed by the map workspace.
nextSteps: Implement a side-by-side comparison slider overlay to allow stakeholders to swipe between historical georeferenced cartography and modern satellite layers.

core.title: Kartino
core.tagline: AI-powered Telegram flashcards rebuilt into a production-ready learning system.

story.summary: An AI-powered language leaning companion that automatically generates, stores and schedules flashcards with context-aware example sentences and translations.
story.problem: Conventional language learning often suffers from quick forgetting cycles, where words learned in textbooks or classes fade rapidly. Standard flashcard applications require heavy manual creation friction and encourage rote memorization of static, repetitive content.
story.solution: A Telegram learning companion using FastAPI and aiogram. It leverages LLMs to generate bespoke flashcards, including dynamic example sentences and conjugations matching the learner's CEFR level, reducing manual typing friction to zero.
story.outcome: Kartino is a production-level learning companion, deliberately designed to be friction-free, easy to use, and close to the learner's daily habits inside Telegram.
highlights: Delivers spaced repetition reviews and LLM-generated conjugation/translation cards directly inside Telegram, keeping the learning loop close to the user's daily message flow.
Rebuilt a raw prototype (originally configured as an N8N automation workflow) into a modular, production-ready Python package with a secure MongoDB database caching layer.
Designed a comprehensive suite of 413 automated tests (unit, integration, and smoke) covering webhook failure modes, database exceptions, and background task resilience.
lessons: Good AI products are not just about the model features; they depend heavily on system responsiveness, trust, and frictionless interaction loops.

core.title: RECORE
core.tagline: Resource Ecosystem for Community Resilience, providing localized crisis tools and knowledge routing.
core.teamContext: Politecnico di Torino RESILIAGE team member; owned end-to-end implementation, platform architecture, and deployment from partner-provided initial design direction

story.summary: An open platform and ecosystem router designed to empower local communities and citizens in real settings by providing free, multilingual access to resilience knowledge, tools, and crisis resources.
story.problem: Communities and individuals in crisis situations lack a central, highly interactive gateway to locate and access essential resilience resources, tools, and documentation in their native languages.
story.solution: A central routing platform that connects all tools in the broader resilience ecosystem. It provides complete native localization across 6 European languages and features a custom tile/card-based catalog with responsive pagination, and interactive hover micro-animations.
story.outcome: RECORE serves as the unified entry portal for the resilience ecosystem, allowing diverse regional pilot sites to access critical knowledge and tools seamlessly.
highlights: Architected and deployed the central router and resource platform for the entire RESILIAGE ecosystem.
Engineered native localization supporting 6 European languages: English, Portuguese, Norwegian, Greek, Turkish, and French.
Designed a modular tile/card-based resource catalog with responsive grid pagination and dynamic content mapping.
Implemented interactive card hover micro-animations and fluid content transition effects to maximize community engagement.
Ensured visual and functional consistency across platform components to align with community design system expectations.
lessons: Designing localized portals for emergency response requires deep decoupling of language assets, ensuring quick translation loading and consistent rendering across complex fluid card grid layouts.
nextSteps: Collaborate with colleagues and project partners to gather requirements, design, and implement the upcoming 'Heritage Drivers' workspace.

core.title: RESILIAGE Replication Toolbox
core.tagline: A guided digital platform for replicating community-based disaster resilience strategies, with role-aware wizard flows, structured knowledge cards, and a CMS-powered resource taxonomy.
core.teamContext: Politecnico di Torino RESILIAGE team member; owned end-to-end design, development, data ingestion pipeline, deployment, and CMS administration

story.summary: A guided platform helping communities adapt and replicate 29+ disaster resilience strategies via a role-aware wizard, interactive knowledge cards, and structured user guides.
story.problem: Consortium partners across 5 pilot sites needed a single digital workspace to publish, discover, and contextually adapt over 29 disaster risk management solutions — spanning monitoring toolkits, training modules, preparedness plans, and awareness campaigns — in a way that's actionable for wildly different stakeholder profiles (first responders, policymakers, citizens, researchers).
story.solution: A React + NestJS full-stack application featuring a multi-step wizard that personalises content by stakeholder role and SyRI (Systemic Resilience Index) dimension. The backend exposes a recursive category -> solution -> info-card -> user-guide data model via RESTful APIs, backed by Prisma/PostgreSQL with S3-hosted file delivery through presigned URLs. A built-in admin panel enables non-technical team members to manage the full content taxonomy, upload files, create info cards with section-linked files, and author multi-step user guides — all without touching code. Content is seeded from parsed markdown documents via automated scripts.
story.outcome: A live, content-managed replication platform serving the RESILIAGE consortium's 18 Horizon Europe partners, providing structured access to 29+ solutions, interactive user guides, and downloadable resources — ready for communities to adapt and replicate DRM strategies in their own contexts.
highlights: Designed a recursive category -> solution -> info-card -> user-guide data model with Prisma, enabling deeply nested DRM taxonomies with structured Q&A sections, step-by-step guides, and linked file attachments per section.
Built a multi-step wizard flow with React Context + useReducer, persisting user state to localStorage, to personalise replication guides based on stakeholder role and SyRI resilience dimension.

Developed a full admin CMS panel enabling non-technical team members to manage the entire content taxonomy: create categories, solutions, upload files to S3 via presigned URLs, author info cards with markdown sections, and build multi-step user guides — all without code.

Engineered a two-phase S3 upload pipeline: the backend generates presigned PutObject URLs, the frontend uploads directly to S3, then confirms back to register the file in PostgreSQL — eliminating backend bandwidth bottlenecks.

Implemented a markdown-driven content pipeline with automated database seeding from structured markdown documents, parsers that extract info-card sections and guide steps, and a manifest-based file registration system.
lessons: Building a content-managed knowledge platform for a research consortium requires treating the data model as a first-class design artifact — the recursive Category -> Solution -> InfoCard -> Section -> LinkedFile graph became the backbone that made both the admin CMS and the public-facing taxonomy navigation coherent.

A multi-step wizard that persists state client-side and generates personalised output creates genuine user engagement, but requires careful decoupling of the guide-building logic from the wizard state to keep the system testable and extensible.
nextSteps: Integrate the personalised wizard output with the backend taxonomy so that the 'Generate Plan' feature pulls live solution data instead of static templates.

core.title: Smart Plant Care
core.tagline: A containerized, modular IoT ecosystem for real-time microclimate monitoring and automated room-level plant care, featuring Flask dashboards, dynamic Docker orchestration, and LLM-powered Telegram diagnostics.
core.teamContext: Developed end-to-end as an academic project to explore containerized IoT microservices, dynamic control logic, and remote interfaces.

story.summary: A highly modular IoT platform designed to automate and monitor indoor microclimates. By connecting device connectors to a containerized microservice backend, the system orchestrates real-time sensor ingestion, automated irrigation control loops, and multi-interface user interactions via Flask and Telegram.
story.problem: Managing multiple distinct plant environments (rooms) usually involves rigid, hardcoded controller scripts. As the number of rooms, sensors, and actuators scales, managing device registration, configuration updates, and asynchronous command loops leads to single points of failure, message loss in communication, and high operational complexity.
story.solution: A decoupled microservice architecture orchestrated via Docker. An MQTT broker handles real-time messages between device connectors and a specialized Device Connector service. A Registry service manages the system catalog (rooms, plants, devices) backed by MongoDB, validated via schemas and models. To solve the scaling problem, a dynamic Control Unit (CU) Manager spins up and configures isolated, room-specific controller containers on the fly, offering REST endpoints to attach or detach rooms. Environmental metrics are forwarded to ThingSpeak for persistent telemetry, while a Flask web dashboard and Telegram bot provide real-time controls, alerts, and LLM-synthesized diagnostics.
story.outcome: A resilient, plug-and-play IoT ecosystem developed for educational purposes that allows new rooms and sensors to be provisioned dynamically without system downtime. The platform handles concurrent sensor streams, maintains automated closed-loop environmental controls (e.g., auto-irrigation based on soil moisture), and delivers AI-driven recommendations directly to users.
highlights: Designed a containerized microservice topology spanning 8+ services (Registry, Adaptor, Connector, CU Manager, Reporter, Telegram Bot, Flask Web, MQTT Broker) to eliminate single points of failure.
Developed a dynamic CU Manager that orchestrates the lifecycle of room-specific Control Units using Docker's SDK, spinning containers up/down dynamically as rooms are added or removed.
Programmed a decoupled MongoDB-backed Catalog Schema featuring structured data models, business logic handlers, and a background Cleaner process to automatically prune stale device registrations.
Implemented an MQTT-to-ThingSpeak ingestion pipeline using a telemetry Adaptor, enabling real-time sensor charts on the dashboard and offline telemetry tracking.

Built a dual-interface user hub: a responsive Flask Web Service for visual dashboard charts and hardware overrides, paired with an interactive Telegram Bot featuring LLM-based microclimate health reports.
lessons: Designing hardware-software bridges requires a strict boundary of decoupling. MQTT provides the perfect message bus, but containerizing the business logic (like room-specific control loops) makes scaling operational capacity seamless.

Telemetry is only as good as its delivery; embedding external charts (ThingSpeak) and layering conversational alerts (Telegram Bot) provides a much higher utility to the end-user than standalone dashboards.
